title: TBM 404: Should Teams Use the Same Tracker?
questions: Should every team use the same tracker to make work visible across the company?
What level of detail is actually reasonable to request from teams without creating bureaucracy?
How can organizations share release information without forcing everyone into the same tooling?
When does cross-team work justify common systems and rituals?
search_terms: should teams use the same tracker
epics initiatives stories as throwaway objects
summary: The post argues that companies can reasonably seek visibility into what teams do and why they exist, but they should avoid forcing identical work trackers. It recommends using broad-strokes priority discussions, interoperable release calendars, and a concrete system of record for deployed changes, while relying on goals/value models/charters as the main interface. It also explains how low trust drives proxy-based oversight and proposes tighter consistency mainly when cross-team work is high.

title: TBM 401: Solving Problems the Hard Way
questions: How do we define the right number of work lanes (not too vague, not too specific) and assign real ownership?
What routines help teams keep tempo, habits, and focus without over-correcting when drift appears?
How can leaders avoid alignment theater and zombie processes while still using lightweight planning artifacts?
How should lanes change over time—when to refactor, pivot, or retire them?
summary: This post argues that effective leadership and strategy execution come from shaping a small set of durable “lanes” with clear ownership, weekly calibration, and habit/tempo coaching—because drift and uncertainty are inevitable. It’s aimed at leaders and team members who want practical operating routines that avoid alignment theater and instead build real learning through collaborative discovery and ongoing refactoring. The piece also critiques overly rigid, frameworkified versions of agile and reframes planning as a continuous sensemaking practice across organizational levels.

title: TBM 387: Lenses
questions: Which lenses should we use in our product and ops planning, and when?
How do customer journey stages map to wardley proximity and capability evolution?
How should team boundaries and team interaction patterns be represented in a product strategy map?
How can we turn a complex visualization into actionable opportunities and options without getting stuck in diagramming?
search_terms: wardley mapping x axis y axis
team topologies X as a Service facilitation collaboration
summary: The post argues that effective product and ops work depends on using multiple “lenses” (customer journey, wardley mapping, team boundaries/interactions, opportunities/options, and execution) rather than relying on a single view. It’s aimed at product teams and operators who build strategy diagrams and need a practical way to connect dimensions and choose which lenses matter at the right time.

title: TBM 386: Understanding Enabling Constraints Using Shape Up (Basecamp)
questions: How do I tell whether a constraint will enable progress or create drag in my organization?
What behaviors does a given constraint encourage or suppress, and what counterbalances are needed?
How can I adapt Shape Up’s constraints to my workplace without copying the mechanics out of context?
What second- and third-order effects should I anticipate before introducing a new constraint?
search_terms: Basecamp Shape Up betting table circuit breaker
summary: This post explores how to use constraints as an “operating system” for product and delivery, using Basecamp’s Shape Up as a concrete case. It’s aimed at product leaders and teams who want practical guidance on designing constraints that focus work, reduce drag, and avoid common failure modes like performative deadlines and backlog bloat. The core emphasis is on constraint intent, cultural support, and the interplay between multiple constraints rather than copying framework mechanics.

title: TBM 369: Sources Of Truth
questions: What reality is being squashed down at my company, and what incentives or power dynamics cause it?
Where do teams do the most exception handling or juggling between competing versions of truth?
How do our tools and systems reflect who is in charge and whose definitions dominate?
How can we minimize copy-pasting without forcing people to mash down reality into a single narrative?
search_terms: portfolio allocation frames capex opex strategic pillars
summary: The post argues that “source of truth” issues in product development are usually symptoms of deeper misalignment about definitions, incentives, and willingness to reconcile multiple realities. It’s aimed at product leaders and teams wrestling with complex relationships among initiatives, epics, releases, and portfolio allocation frames, and who want to reduce copy-paste without erasing necessary nuance. The piece highlights adoption limits of overly complex data models and offers questions for surfacing diverse truths while managing cognitive load.

title: TBM 367: The Wicked "Big Picture" Loop
questions: How do we prevent the big picture loop where simplified dashboards drift from reality?
What should leaders do when teams are told to simplify but reality keeps getting hidden?
How can we design lightweight constraints that surface hot spots without killing autonomy?
What accountability cadence helps teams close loops instead of back-channeling?
search_terms: OKR cycle missed because of simplification
how to stop optics game leadership
summary: The post explains a recurring organizational failure mode where executives repeatedly ask for a simplified “big picture,” teams filter reality to comply, and leadership eventually disengages until a crisis forces the truth back into view—then the cycle restarts. It is aimed at product and leadership teams trying to improve roadmap/OKR execution by designing better constraints and accountability mechanisms (including coaching-like cadence) to keep planning aligned with reality.

title: TBM 330: (Un)common Sense Playbook
questions: How can an engineering manager stabilize a struggling team without losing momentum for experimentation?
What enables a commitment pause or “time out” to succeed with stakeholders and the team?
Why do otherwise competent managers struggle to get buy-in on basic changes?
When should the problem be framed as environment and conditions rather than individual manager skill?
search_terms: how to stabilize a team temporary pause
time out social capital stakeholder management
trust and psychological safety after layoffs
summary: The post outlines a “common sense” engineering management playbook for turning around struggling teams: align on the why, engage customers, stabilize via a temporary pause, set metrics and cadence, and gradually reintroduce work. It’s aimed at engineering leaders and managers who want to understand why these tactics sometimes fail despite being straightforward. The author argues that execution depends heavily on environmental conditions—optics pressure, toxic influence, trust, and partner-team capacity—not just manager skill.

title: TBM 325: Skeptoptimism – Thinking Slow, Acting Fast
questions: How do I think slow without falling into analysis paralysis?
What does it mean to plan through experimentation rather than abstract calculation?
How can I communicate skepticism and optimism so others don’t misread deep dives as pessimism or delay?
How do I ensure my team is truly aligned before I rely on their ability to pivot and recover?
summary: The post argues that “thinking slow, acting fast” should be practiced as active experimentation and experience, not passive deliberation. It’s aimed at leaders and team members who identify as skeptoptimists and want to navigate the tension between deep risk exploration and rapid execution, including how to communicate optimism so others don’t mistake it for waste or pessimism.

title: TBM 294: Close the Loop. Stop the Drift
questions: What should we be stubborn about to ensure we don’t let problems fester or drift over time?
How can we build shared understanding of the signals that things are slipping?
Where are our processes running us instead of helping us execute and course-correct?
What dissonance or open loops are we avoiding, and how can we close them earlier?
search_terms: close the loop on open questions
team drift and how to stop it
summary: This post argues that high-performing teams stay effective by actively closing feedback loops: revisiting open threads, integrating perspectives, surfacing dissonance, and escalating early when things break. It is aimed at engineering/product leaders and team members who want practical ways to prevent execution drift, avoid optics-driven incentives, and maintain course-correction under change.

title: TBM 292: Friction, Documentation, and Product Tools
questions: Do we have state history, or are we only looking at the current state?
Why do our plans change, and can we explain the variance behind the say/do gap?
Are our tools creating information asymmetries between leaders and teams?
Before adopting complex products, would simple documents and migration be sufficient?
search_terms: manual documentation vs project management tools
OKRs roadmap tracking without fancy tools
summary: This post argues that teams should use simple, static documentation and manual repetition—specifically the bullet-journaling practice of migration—to preserve state history and force reflection. It connects migration and state history to better understanding of the say/do gap, organizational learning, and information asymmetries, and it recommends trying low-tech tools before adopting complex work-tracking products.

title: Octopus Careers & Throwaway Stickies with Chris Butler
questions: How do you find your “tribe” inside large companies when your interests don’t fit a single career track?
What’s the difference between strategy and a plan or roadmap, and why does it matter?
How can leaders introduce new collaboration and decision-making practices without triggering cultural backlash?
Why do some cultures demand perfect documentation and consensus, and how does that affect strategic rehearsal?
search_terms: critical uncertainties two by two scenario planning
summary: This post is an interview focused on how product operations and product leaders can drive change inside large organizations while preserving their own multi-disciplinary identity. It’s for practitioners who want practical ways to rehearse strategy under uncertainty (e.g., Wardley mapping, design fiction, scenario planning) and to build feedback-rich team cultures without micromanaging. The discussion contrasts consensus-driven corporate cultures with communities of practice that treat artifacts as disposable learning tools.

title: TBM 252: How to Troubleshoot Status Updates and Syncs
questions: Why do our weekly status updates keep repeating the same issues without real progress?
How can we make syncs more actionable instead of delivery-focused or laggy?
What should we do when priorities keep changing and updates feel like restarting?
How do we ensure updates create reflection, learning, and a record rather than venting?
search_terms: weekly goals and weekly review rituals
summary: This post helps teams diagnose why status updates and regular sync rituals lose value over time and become repetitive, performative, or disconnected from real progress. It’s aimed at managers and team members who run weekly goals/reviews and want practical fixes to improve relevance, reflection, and learning. The core message is that adoption depends on perceived value and actionable progress signals, not on the specific process or format.

title: TBM 251: Scale vs. Efficiency
questions: When is strategic inefficiency worth the gamble?
What are good examples of companies that won by being slower or less efficient than competitors?
How can you distinguish normal growing pains from core efficiency failures in your organization?
What clues indicate that a team’s strategic inefficiency is actually going the wrong direction?
search_terms: economies of scale and operational excellence
summary: This post explores how scale and operational efficiency interact, arguing that efficiency declines with growth are not inevitable and that investor outcomes can be driven by factors beyond day-to-day efficiency. It introduces a distinction between normal growing pains and “core efficiency,” then explains how teams may intentionally tolerate certain inefficiencies to pursue higher-priority strategic bets. The piece is aimed at leaders and operators navigating growth, prioritization, and execution tradeoffs in scaling organizations.

title: TBM 243: Capital Inefficiency
questions: Is our focus on developer productivity really about performance, or is it masking capital inefficiency?
Where are we overstaffed or understaffed relative to market opportunity, architecture, and feedback systems?
How did low interest rates shape our past decisions, and what should we do differently now?
Why do productivity and cost-cutting conversations become blame and identity battles instead of problem-solving?
summary: The post argues that “productivity” and “efficiency” debates in tech are often surface-level proxies for deeper capital inefficiency in how companies fund, staff, and structure work. It’s aimed at leaders and practitioners who are navigating layoffs, shifting interest-rate realities, and stakeholder blame dynamics, and it recommends using systems thinking to address capital efficiency across org design, underinvestment, and feedback/enablement—not just headcount or near-term ROI.

title: TBM 238: Spinning Plates
questions: Why does an optimization mindset make product teams less efficient instead of more efficient?
How do teams accidentally set up efforts to fail when they try to do more with the same capacity?
What are enabling constraints, and how can they improve flow and delivery?
When should leaders avoid stretch goals and instead shoot low to build momentum?
search_terms: stretch goals when normal goals are missed
too many drivers too many constraints
summary: The post argues that humans naturally optimize for ROI and efficiency, but this can backfire in product development by increasing constraints, drivers, and coordination overhead—ultimately slowing innovation and delivery. It helps product managers and leaders recognize when plans become unwinnable and offers practical countermeasures like radical enabling constraints, extreme focus, and “shooting low” rather than relying on stretch goals.

title: TBM 229: Winnable and Unwinnable Games (Part 1)
questions: How do you tell when your team is playing an unwinnable game?
What changes (drivers, limiting constraints, floats, enabling constraints) make a work system winnable?
How should you introduce a “right” way of working when trust and psychological safety are low?
How do you resolve clashes between different games played by leaders, managers, and engineers?
search_terms: drivers limiting constraints floats enabling constraints
say do commitment scope deadline far in future
low trust environment agile proxy metrics
North Star Framework autonomy meaningful goals
summary: This post reframes product development and delivery frameworks as “games” that can be designed to feel winnable, motivating, and valuable to customers and the business. It explores why teams end up in unwinnable or low-value games—especially when constraints, drivers, and trust dynamics are misaligned—and how multiple organizational “games” clash across roles. It’s aimed at product leaders, engineering managers, and teams trying to improve delivery outcomes by redesigning the work system.

title: TBM 205: "Process" vs. Systems & Habits
questions: How do we distinguish valuable ways of working from performative, non-value-add process?
What incentives cause teams to fall into reactive planning and learned helplessness?
How can leaders build habits and systems that enable autonomy while staying aligned to company strategy?
Why do big-bang offsites fail to sustain improvements after returning to business as usual?
search_terms: how to break a wicked loop in organizations
summary: The post contrasts two organizational approaches to planning: a compliance-driven, annual “process” that produces performative rushing versus a habit-based “system” that continuously shapes ideas and keeps roadmaps aligned. It’s aimed at leaders and teams trying to improve strategy execution, morale, and adaptability by replacing reactive governance with human-centric operating habits and incremental change.

title: TBM 38/52: Navigating the Product Leadership Fog
questions: How do I handle the gap between corporate strategy and day-to-day plans when priorities keep changing?
What should I do when both strategic thinkers and get-it-done thinkers are frustrated and misaligned?
How do I rebuild trust and psychological safety when the team is in blame mode and people are leaving?
What does “good enough” strategy look like when we don’t have time to think strategically?
search_terms: degrading trust psychological safety product team
how to relieve teams from filling the void
summary: This post offers guidance for product leaders navigating organizational confusion during rapid growth and constant priority shifts. It frames the core issue as a “leadership fog” driven by degrading trust and psychological safety, and it distinguishes the different needs of strategic thinkers versus get-it-done thinkers. It’s aimed at leaders who feel pressure to produce perfect strategy and plan but need practical steps to restore trust, reduce blame, and create cadence with a good-enough strategy.

title: Free training sessions (to help me become a better trainer)
questions: How can I become a better trainer by changing my facilitation style?
What learning outcomes should I use to design short training sessions?
How do we communicate and deploy strategy so teams stay aligned?
How do we choose metrics and measure the impact of our work?
search_terms: training from the back of the room
how to be a better trainer
summary: The post offers 30 short, guided training sessions (55 minutes) for leaders in product, design, engineering, and analytics who want to improve how they teach and how they execute strategy. It is inspired by the idea of “training from the back of the room,” emphasizing learners doing most of the talking while the trainer guides toward specific learning outcomes. The sessions cover strategy development, communication, measurement, prioritization, alignment, and team structuring.

title: TBM 29B/52: Asking for Feedback and Pushback
questions: How should I interpret different kinds of feedback when my priorities conflict?
What do hesitation, enthusiasm, and problem-only feedback mean in a strategy workshop?
How do I get from identifying constraints to producing a workable prioritization plan?
How does team experience and organizational dysfunction change what “good feedback” looks like?
search_terms: how to interpret hesitation in teams
summary: This post is for product leaders who want to solicit feedback and pushback when their strategy contains competing priorities. It outlines how different experience levels produce different responses—from barely listening to verbalizing constraints and proposing a prioritization path—and argues that leaders should treat these responses as signals rather than expect uniform, actionable feedback. It also emphasizes that feedback quality depends on the leader’s environment-building and the team’s context, not just individual talent.

title: TBM 18/52: We Need Someone Who Has Done "It" Before
questions: Why do we keep failing to hire for the same role and end up with a revolving door?
How can leaders define “it” when they can’t explain the context or the underlying playbooks?
When should we copy, mix, adapt, or invent playbooks for a new hire to succeed?
How can we use wardley mapping to clarify hiring needs and reduce misalignment?
search_terms: hire someone who has done it before
copy and paste vs invent playbooks
summary: This post addresses why “hire someone who has done it before” often produces repeated failures and revolving-door turnover. It argues that the real issue is leaders and candidates operating with mismatched implicit knowledge about what “it” actually entails, and it proposes classifying work into playbook types. For leaders, hiring managers, and org designers, it recommends using wardley mapping to visualize the capability network and to guide both hiring conversations and candidate evaluation.

title: Make it Hard(er) to Say Yes
questions: How can I improve my ability to say no without being rude?
What process changes can prevent teams from committing to the first good idea?
How do forcing functions reduce bias in meetings and execution decisions?
Why can annual planning make it easier to say yes to the wrong work?
search_terms: make it harder to say yes
how to say no without saying no
summary: This post argues that instead of focusing on how to refuse requests, teams should design the process so that saying yes requires conscious consideration. It uses the concept of forcing functions to show how meeting dynamics and recency bias can produce premature commitments, and contrasts ineffective rigidity (big-bang annual planning) with healthier, feedback-driven yesses. It’s aimed at leaders and collaborators who want better decision quality while maintaining professional respect.

title: TBM 15/52: A Tale of Two Meetings
questions: Why do some meetings produce action items that never get executed?
What structural constraints cause teams to drift into inefficient planning and committee work?
How can an organization redesign meetings to improve decision traceability and follow-through?
What metrics indicate whether change efforts are actually improving execution?
search_terms: meeting effectiveness next steps not happening
how to create more execution meetings
summary: The post contrasts two meeting archetypes to explain why some teams repeatedly fail to translate brainstorming into executed next steps. It argues that meeting dysfunction is often structural—driven by dependencies, WIP, outdated roadmaps, and information/ownership gaps—leading to a drift into failure. Intended for leaders and product/engineering teams seeking practical ways to shift energy and information flow so more decisions turn into real execution.

title: TBM 46/52: Annual Planning (Wicked Problem)
questions: Why can’t product teams do more continuous planning instead of annual planning?
What makes annual planning a wicked problem in real organizations?
How do execution mode, feedback latency, and output-centricity undermine strategy work?
What small experiments could replace the annual planning ritual with more frequent planning cycles?
search_terms: reduce feedback loop latency product planning
summary: The post argues that while continuous planning is theoretically achievable and beneficial, annual planning persists because it functions like a wicked problem shaped by organizational constraints. It helps product and strategy leaders understand the real-world drivers—execution mode, slow feedback, output pressure, budgeting and executive timing—that make frequent planning difficult, and suggests experimenting with recurring planning time blocks.

title: TBM 42/53: The Annual Planning Dance
questions: Is the annual planning dance the best or only way to align strategy and budgets?
Where does annual planning typically break down—during the handoff from leadership to departments?
How can teams avoid premature convergence and the messy middle during strategy deployment?
What planning approach works better than a painful 2+ month annual cycle?
search_terms: strategy handoff between leadership and teams
summary: The post examines why annual planning often becomes a high-drama, siloed diverge-converge process with negotiation and deadlines, and argues it doesn’t have to be inevitable. It’s aimed at leaders and product/strategy practitioners who want a better way to translate vague top-level direction into workable departmental plans by improving the early handoff and enabling shared sense-making. The core focus is preventing the 'messy middle' and 'premature convergence' through a 'start together' approach.
